meta.title: Peshitta Aramaic IPA Pronunciation Guide
meta.purpose: Machine-readable IPA-based pronunciation guide for Peshitta Syriac Aramaic covering BOTH script systems: Syriac script (Estrangela/Serto, U+0700–U+074F) and Block Aramaic / Hebrew square script (U+0590–U+05FF). All pronunciation data is encoded in the International Phonetic Alphabet (IPA, 2015 revision) as the primary and only system.

block_aramaic.name: Block Aramaic (Hebrew square script / Ktav Ashuri)
block_aramaic.note: Same script as Biblical Aramaic and Biblical Hebrew; the language is Syriac Aramaic

meta.tradition_focus: Peshitta New Testament (both Eastern/Madnhaya and Western/Serto traditions)

phonological_framework.description: Consonants classified by IPA place of articulation, manner of articulation, and voicing. Vowels classified by height, backness, and rounding per the IPA vowel chart.

peshitta_new_testament.description: The complete Peshitta New Testament in four parallel text variants

variants.description: Voweled Block Aramaic with niqqud pointing

notes: Syriac and Block Aramaic are written right-to-left
The Peshitta is the standard Syriac Bible translation, used by all Syriac Christian traditions
Western (Serto) and Eastern (Madnhaya) traditions differ in pronunciation and vowel notation
Block Aramaic texts use Hebrew-style niqqud vocalization, not Syriac vowel dots
All IPA transcriptions use the standard 2015 IPA chart notation
Phonemic transcriptions use / / slashes; phonetic transcriptions use [ ] brackets
Suprasegmentals: /ː/ marks vowel length; /ˈ/ marks primary stress; /ˤ/ marks pharyngealization
Syriac Resh = alveolar trill /r/ (NOT uvular /ʁ/ as in Tiberian Hebrew)

phonological_inventory.description: The complete phonemic inventory of Classical Syriac as used in the Peshitta, organized by IPA categories. Shared across both script systems.

consonant_phonemes.description: All consonant phonemes of Classical Syriac arranged by place and manner of articulation
consonant_phonemes.note: 22 letters yield 31 distinct phonemes: 16 non-Begadkephat consonants + 6 Begadkephat hard (plosive) + 6 Begadkephat soft (fricative) + labial-velar /w/ + palatal /j/ = 31

bilabial.notes: /b/ and /β/ are allophones of Beth; /p/ and /ɸ/ (or /f/) are allophones of Pe

labiodental.notes: Modern realizations of soft Beth [v] and soft Pe [f]; classical forms may have been bilabial [β] and [ɸ]

dental.notes: /ð/ is soft Dalath; /θ/ is soft Taw

alveolar.notes: /tˤ/ is Teth (emphatic); /sˤ/ is Tsade (emphatic). /d/ and /ð/ are allophones of Dalath; /t/ and /θ/ are allophones of Taw. Resh = alveolar trill /r/.

velar.notes: /ɡ/ and /ɣ/ are allophones of Gamal; /k/ and /x/ are allophones of Kaph

ipa_consonant_chart.description: IPA consonant chart for Classical Syriac (rows = manner, columns = place). Voiceless precedes voiced in each cell.
ipa_consonant_chart.note: /w/ is labial-velar and not shown in this simplified chart. Modern realizations: /β/→[v], /ɸ/→[f].

vowel_phonemes.description: The vowel phonemes of Classical Syriac, with Eastern (7 vowels) and Western (5 vowels) inventories
vowel_phonemes.note: Eastern Syriac preserves a 7-vowel system; Western Syriac merged several vowels to yield 5

ipa_vowel_chart.description: IPA vowel quadrilateral for Classical Syriac
ipa_vowel_chart.note: Western merged /ɛ/+/e/→/e/ and /ɑ/→/ɔ/. The Zqapha vowel is /ɑ/ Eastern vs /ɔ/ Western.

eastern_phonemes.distribution: Open back vowel; corresponds to Western /ɔ/

consonants.description: The 22 consonant letters of the Peshitta Syriac alphabet in both Syriac and Block Aramaic scripts. IPA values are shared (same language, same phonemes).
consonants.effective_phoneme_note: 22 letters → 31 phonemes due to 6 Begadkephat pairs (hard/soft) + base consonants

begadkephat.description: The six Begadkephat letters (בגדכפת / ܒܓܕܟܦܬ) each have two pronunciations: hard (plosive, with Qushshaya/dagesh) and soft (fricative, with Rukkakha/no dagesh). Dual-track: Syriac marks hardness with dots above/below; Block Aramaic uses dagesh.
begadkephat.rule: After a vowel sound (post-vocalic position), Begadkephat letters are soft (fricative). After silence or a consonant, they are hard (plosive). This is the spirantization rule.

letters.name: Beth/Bet
letters.soft_description: voiced labiodental fricative (classical: voiced bilabial fricative /β/)

letters.name: Pe
letters.soft_description: voiceless labiodental fricative (classical: voiceless bilabial fricative /ɸ/)

dagesh_system.description: The dagesh system applies to Block Aramaic (Hebrew square script) texts of the Peshitta. Syriac script uses Qushshaya/Rukkakha dots instead. In Block Aramaic voweled texts (BlockVersion_Vowels/), dagesh appears as a dot inside the consonant letter.
dagesh_system.syriac_equivalent: Qushshaya (ܩܘܫܝܐ) — dot above letter for hard pronunciation

dagesh_lene.description: Marks the hard (plosive) pronunciation of Begadkephat letters: בּ גּ דּ כּ פּ תּ
dagesh_lene.rule: Appears when the letter is not preceded by a vowel sound (word-initial, after consonant, after shva nach)

dagesh_forte.description: Marks gemination (consonant doubling) in Block Aramaic voweled texts
dagesh_forte.applies_to: Most consonants except gutturals (א ה ח ע) and Resh (ר)
dagesh_forte.note: In Peshitta Block Aramaic texts, dagesh forte usage follows Hebrew conventions applied to Aramaic morphology

dagesh_system.disambiguation_rule: In Begadkephat letters, dagesh lene and dagesh forte are the same grapheme (dot inside letter). Context determines which: if the letter could only be hard (word-initial), it is dagesh lene; if preceded by a vowel where doubling applies, it is dagesh forte.

mappiq.description: A dot inside final He (הּ) indicating that He is consonantal /h/, not a mater lectionis
mappiq.note: Same grapheme as dagesh but functionally distinct; applies to final He only

vowel_diacritics.description: Two parallel vowel diacritical systems for the Peshitta: (1) Syriac vowel dots (Eastern Madnhaya / Western Serto traditions) and (2) Hebrew-style niqqud pointing used in Block Aramaic voweled texts.

syriac_vowel_system.description: Syriac vocalization uses dots placed above or below letters. The Eastern (Madnhaya) system uses dot pairs; the Western (Serto) system uses small Greek-derived letters.

western_serto.name: Pthakha
western_serto.note: Open central vowel (same IPA as Eastern)

block_aramaic_niqqud_system.description: Block Aramaic voweled texts (BlockVersion_Vowels/) use Hebrew-style niqqud pointing. The vowel signs are from the Hebrew Unicode block and are applied to Aramaic text in Hebrew square script.
block_aramaic_niqqud_system.note: Niqqud values when applied to Peshitta Aramaic may differ from their Biblical Hebrew values because the underlying language is Syriac Aramaic.

shva_system.description: The shva system in Block Aramaic voweled texts of the Peshitta. Shva (שְׁוָא) represents either a reduced vowel /ə/ (shva na/mobile) or silence (shva nach/quiescent).
shva_system.applies_to: Block Aramaic voweled texts (BlockVersion_Vowels/) only. Syriac script does not use shva.

shva_na.name: Shva na (mobile/vocal)
shva_na.description: Pronounced as a mid-central reduced vowel
rules: At the beginning of a word
After another shva (the second of two consecutive shvas)
Under a letter with dagesh forte
After a long vowel in an open syllable

shva_nach.name: Shva nach (quiescent/silent)
shva_nach.description: Not pronounced; marks the end of a closed syllable
rules: At the end of a word
The first of two consecutive shvas (except word-final)
After a short vowel in a closed syllable

shva_system.note: Shva rules in Peshitta Block Aramaic follow the Hebrew conventions applied to Aramaic morphology. Some contexts may differ from Biblical Hebrew shva rules due to different syllable structures in Syriac Aramaic.

hataf_vowels.description: Ultra-short (reduced) vowels used under guttural consonants in Block Aramaic voweled texts. Gutturals (א ה ח ע) prefer hataf vowels over plain shva.

vowels.name: Hataf Segol
vowels.description: Ultra-short open-mid front vowel; replaces shva na under gutturals

vowels.name: Hataf Patach
vowels.description: Ultra-short open front vowel; the most common hataf vowel

vowels.name: Hataf Qamats
vowels.description: Ultra-short open-mid back rounded vowel; under gutturals before /ɔ/ or /o/

shin_sin_distinction.description: In Block Aramaic (Hebrew square script), the letter Shin (ש) can represent two phonemes: Shin /ʃ/ (postalveolar fricative, dot upper-right) and Sin /s/ (alveolar fricative, dot upper-left). In Syriac script, Shin (ܫ) is a single letter representing /ʃ/ only; the /s/ phoneme is represented by Semkath (ܣ).
shin_sin_distinction.applies_to: Block Aramaic script; Syriac script has a single Shin letter

sin.description: Alveolar fricative (same phoneme as Samekh ס)

syriac_equivalence.note: Syriac distinguishes /ʃ/ and /s/ with separate letters. Block Aramaic uses one base letter with distinguishing dots.

shin_sin_distinction.note: In Peshitta Block Aramaic voweled texts, the Shin/Sin distinction may or may not be consistently marked. Where it appears, Shin dot = /ʃ/ and Sin dot = /s/.

final_form_letters.description: Block Aramaic (Hebrew square script) uses special final forms for 5 letters when they appear at the end of a word. Syriac script does NOT have final forms — all letters have the same form regardless of position.
final_form_letters.syriac_note: Syriac script has no final letter forms. Letters connect differently in Syriac cursive (Serto) based on position, but the base character is the same.
final_form_letters.note: Peshitta Block Aramaic texts in the source corpus may or may not use final forms consistently. The BlockVersion/ texts in this corpus do NOT use final forms (all letters use medial forms throughout).

consonant_vowel_ipa_matrix.description: Consonant + vowel IPA combinations for all 22 Syriac consonants. IPA values are shared across both scripts. Uses the Eastern 7-vowel system; Western variants can be derived by applying the Eastern→Western vowel mappings.

diacritical_marks.description: Diacritical marks used in Peshitta manuscripts and printed editions. Presented for both Syriac and Block Aramaic scripts.

syriac_diacritics.name: Qushshaya (ܩܘܫܝܐ)
syriac_diacritics.function: Marks hard (plosive) pronunciation of Begadkephat letter

syriac_diacritics.name: Rukkakha (ܪܘܟܟܐ)
syriac_diacritics.function: Marks soft (fricative) pronunciation of Begadkephat letter

syriac_diacritics.name: Seyame (ܣܝܡܐ)
syriac_diacritics.ipa_effect: No direct phonetic effect; morphological marker
syriac_diacritics.note: Unique to Syriac script; Block Aramaic has no direct equivalent

syriac_diacritics.name: Linea Occultans
syriac_diacritics.function: Marks a silent (quiescent) letter — the letter is written but not pronounced
syriac_diacritics.position: Small line above or below the letter
syriac_diacritics.note: Common on silent Alaph and other mater lectionis letters

syriac_diacritics.name: Mhaggyānā (ܡܗܓܝܢܐ)
syriac_diacritics.function: Marks a vowelless consonant or emphasizes pronunciation
syriac_diacritics.ipa_effect: Varies; typically indicates the consonant is pronounced without a following vowel

block_aramaic_diacritics.name: Meteg
block_aramaic_diacritics.function: Secondary stress marker; indicates shva na or clarifies vowel length

block_aramaic_diacritics.name: Maqaf
block_aramaic_diacritics.function: Word connector (hyphen); joins words into a prosodic unit
block_aramaic_diacritics.ipa_effect: Stress shift to the final word of the unit

block_aramaic_diacritics.name: Rafe
block_aramaic_diacritics.function: Marks soft (fricative) pronunciation of Begadkephat — opposite of dagesh
block_aramaic_diacritics.note: Rare in printed texts; dagesh absence typically implies soft pronunciation

suprasegmentals.description: Prosodic and suprasegmental features of Classical Syriac pronunciation

stress.description: Primary stress in Classical Syriac falls on the penultimate (second-to-last) syllable in most words. Some suffixed and construct forms shift stress to the final syllable.
exceptions: Monosyllabic words: stress falls on the single syllable
Words with heavy final syllable (CVCC or CVːC): stress may shift to the final syllable

gemination.description: Consonant length (gemination) is phonemic in Eastern Syriac and indicated by doubling in IPA
gemination.eastern_tradition: Gemination preserved; phonemic contrast between single and doubled consonants
gemination.western_tradition: Gemination largely lost; historical geminates simplified to single consonants
gemination.note: In Block Aramaic voweled texts, gemination is marked by dagesh forte

vowel_length.description: Vowel length distinctions in Classical Syriac
vowel_length.ipa_notation: ː after the vowel for long vowels
vowel_length.note: Vowel length correlates with syllable type: short vowels in closed syllables, long vowels in open syllables

pharyngealization.description: Emphatic consonants /tˤ/ and /sˤ/ are produced with pharyngealization, which spreads to adjacent vowels
pharyngealization.vowel_effect: Adjacent vowels are backed and lowered: /a/ → [ɑ], /i/ → [ɪ], /u/ → [ʊ]

diphthongs.description: Diphthong combinations in Classical Syriac, shared across both scripts

diphthongs.example_context: When Yudh follows a Pthakha vowel
When Waw follows a Pthakha vowel
When Yudh follows an Rbasa vowel

diphthongs.note: Diphthongs in Syriac are relatively limited compared to Biblical Hebrew. Most apparent diphthongs have monophthongized historically (/aj/ → /eː/, /aw/ → /oː/).

syllable_structure.description: Syllable structure patterns in Classical Syriac. This section is unique to the Peshitta guide.

onset.description: C or CC — one or two consonants

nucleus.description: V — a single vowel (monophthong or diphthong)

coda.description: C or CC — one or two consonants

constraints: Short vowels /a/, /ɛ/, /i/ typically occur in closed syllables (with a coda consonant)
Long vowels /aː/, /eː/, /iː/, /oː/, /uː/ may occur in open or closed syllables
Onset clusters are limited; most onsets are single consonants
Coda clusters of two consonants are common in word-final position

phonological_rules.description: Active phonological processes in Classical Syriac

rules.ipa_notation: /b d ɡ k p t/ → [v ð ɣ x f θ] / V_
rules.description: Begadkephat letters become fricatives after a vowel (post-vocalic spirantization)
Nun assimilates to the place of articulation of a following consonant, especially before dental and alveolar consonants
The Taw of the Ethpeel/Ethpaal reflexive stems assimilates to following sibilants (/s/, /z/, /sˤ/, /ʃ/)
Alaph frequently becomes silent (quiescent), especially in medial and final positions. Marked by Linea Occultans in Syriac script.
examples: final Alaph in emphatic state nouns is typically silent

rules.ipa_notation: V → [ə] or ∅ / in pretonic open syllables
rules.description: Short vowels in unstressed open syllables may reduce to schwa or be elided
Emphatic consonants cause adjacent vowels to be backed and lowered
Waw and Yudh alternate between consonantal (/w/, /j/) and vocalic (/u/o/, /i/e/) realizations based on syllable position

rules.rule: Gemination of consonants after short vowels
rules.ipa_notation: C → Cː / V̆_ (in Pa''el, Aph'el stems)
rules.description: The middle radical is geminated in Pa''el and related verb stems (Eastern tradition preserves gemination; Western tends to simplify)
Final He is often silent, serving as a mater lectionis for the preceding vowel

rules.ipa_notation: V → Vː / when a following consonant is lost
rules.description: When a consonant (especially Alaph or He) is lost, the preceding vowel lengthens to compensate
When Ethpeel prefix /t/ precedes a sibilant, metathesis may occur
Geminate consonants are simplified in absolute word-final position

east_west_ipa_differences.description: Systematic pronunciation differences between Eastern (Madnhaya) and Western (Serto) traditions, expressed in IPA. This section is unique to the Peshitta guide.

differences.eastern_ipa: 7 phonemes: /a ɑ ɛ e i o u/

vocalization_traditions.description: Three vocalization systems used to represent Peshitta vowels: Eastern Madnhaya dots, Western Serto marks, and Block Aramaic (Hebrew-style) niqqud.

traditions.name: Eastern (Madnhaya)
traditions.description: Uses pairs of dots above and below letters to indicate vowels. Developed in the Church of the East tradition.
traditions.gemination: Preserved; marked by Mhaggyānā or contextual rules

traditions.name: Western (Serto)
traditions.description: Uses small Greek-derived vowel letters placed above or below consonants. Developed in the Syriac Orthodox tradition.

traditions.name: Block Aramaic niqqud
traditions.description: Uses Hebrew Tiberian-style niqqud pointing applied to Aramaic text in Hebrew script. Found in study editions and interlinear Bibles.
traditions.gemination: Marked by dagesh forte (dot inside consonant)
traditions.key_feature: Hebrew niqqud applied to Syriac Aramaic morphology; vowel values may differ from Biblical Hebrew

cantillation_overview.description: Syriac liturgical accent marks (qeryānā / qonunā) used in Peshitta manuscripts for chanting and reading. Less codified than the Hebrew cantillation (teamim) system.
cantillation_overview.note: Syriac cantillation is primarily an oral tradition with regional variation. The accent marks in manuscripts serve as guides for chanting patterns rather than a strict grammatical system.

syriac_accents.name: Pāsōqā (ܦܣܘܩܐ)
syriac_accents.function: Verse divider; marks the end of a verse

syriac_accents.name: Zāwgā Elpāyā (ܙܘܓܐ ܐܠܦܝܐ)
syriac_accents.function: Phrase divider; marks the primary division within a verse
syriac_accents.ipa_effect: Major pause; slight pitch rise before pause

syriac_accents.name: ʿElyāyā (ܥܠܝܝܐ)
syriac_accents.function: Minor accent mark, above the line

cantillation_overview.block_aramaic_note: Block Aramaic texts of the Peshitta typically do not include cantillation marks. Hebrew cantillation (teamim) is not applied to Peshitta Block texts.
cantillation_overview.comparison_with_hebrew: Syriac cantillation is simpler and less codified than the Hebrew te'amim system. Hebrew has 28+ distinct cantillation marks with strict hierarchical rules; Syriac has fewer marks with more regional variation.

cross_reference_to_aramaic_and_hebrew.description: Three-way cross-reference between Peshitta Syriac, Biblical Aramaic, and Biblical Hebrew. Documents phonological correspondences, shared features, and key differences.
shared_features: All three are Semitic languages written right-to-left
All use the Begadkephat spirantization rule (6 letters with hard/soft allophones)
All have guttural consonants: Aleph/Alaph /ʔ/, He /h/, Kheth/Heth /ħ/, Ayin /ʕ/
All have emphatic consonants: Teth /tˤ/, Tsade/Tsadi /sˤ/
All have pharyngealized (emphatic) consonants that spread to adjacent vowels
Triconsonantal root morphology is shared across all three languages

key_differences.biblical_hebrew: /ʁ/ (uvular fricative, Tiberian reconstruction per Geoffrey Khan)
key_differences.note: Syriac and Biblical Aramaic preserve the older Semitic alveolar /r/

key_differences.peshitta: Eastern: 7 vowels /a ɑ ɛ e i o u/; Western: 5 vowels /a ɔ e i u/
key_differences.biblical_aramaic: Tiberian: 7 vowels /a ɛ e i ɔ o u/
key_differences.biblical_hebrew: Tiberian: 7 vowels /a ɛ e i ɔ o u/
key_differences.note: Peshitta Eastern and Tiberian systems have the same number of vowels but different distributions

key_differences.peshitta: Syriac script (U+0700–U+074F) primary; Block Aramaic (U+0590–U+05FF) secondary
key_differences.note: Block Aramaic Peshitta shares the same script as Biblical Aramaic and Hebrew
Block Aramaic Peshitta uses the same niqqud system as Biblical Aramaic/Hebrew

key_differences.peshitta: Syriac: separate letters (Shin ܫ /ʃ/, Semkath ܣ /s/); Block: dot system (שׁ /ʃ/, שׂ /s/)
key_differences.note: All three distinguish /ʃ/ and /s/ but Syriac uses separate letters

key_differences.peshitta: Syriac: none; Block Aramaic: uses Hebrew final forms (ך ם ן ף ץ)
key_differences.biblical_aramaic: Hebrew final forms (ך ם ן ף ץ)
key_differences.biblical_hebrew: Hebrew final forms (ך ם ן ף ץ)
key_differences.note: Syriac script has no final forms; Block Aramaic follows Hebrew convention

key_differences.peshitta: Syriac: contextual / Mhaggyānā; Block: dagesh forte
key_differences.note: Eastern Syriac preserves gemination; Western has largely lost it

key_differences.biblical_hebrew: Prefix /ha-/ with dagesh forte on following consonant
key_differences.note: Aramaic languages (Peshitta + Biblical Aramaic) use suffixed definiteness; Hebrew uses a prefix

sample_words_ipa.description: 16 sample words extracted from the Peshitta source corpus in all four script variants: consonantal Syriac, voweled Syriac, consonantal Block Aramaic, and voweled Block Aramaic. Each word includes IPA transcription for both Eastern and Western traditions, source reference, codepoint verification, and segmental decomposition derived from the word field.
sample_words_ipa.source_note: All words are byte-sliced from actual source text files in the Peshitta/ folder. No characters were manually typed.

unicode_reference.description: Unicode codepoint reference for all characters used in this guide, covering both Syriac (U+0700–U+074F) and Hebrew/Block Aramaic (U+0590–U+05FF) Unicode blocks.

hebrew_block.note: Used for Block Aramaic (Hebrew square script) representation of Peshitta text

numerical_values.description: Gematria (numerical values) of the Syriac and Block Aramaic alphabets. Both systems use the same traditional Semitic numerical assignment.
